text: The surgeon demonstrates the port placement for a robotic right middle lobectomy, using two twelve-millimeter and two eight-millimeter ports. The eight-millimeter camera port is placed first at the junction between the sternum and xiphoid, approximately at the seventh intercostal space. The medial staple port is positioned one intercostal space higher, typically between the sixth and seventh ribs. The twelve-millimeter air seal port is triangulated between the medial staple port and the camera port, while the assistant port is placed low at the junction between the diaphragm and chest, providing ample maneuvering space.

The surgeon begins the lobectomy by dividing the inferior pulmonary ligament, using a sponge roll to elevate the diaphragm surface of the right lower lobe for tension. Station 9R lymph nodes are harvested, and the inferior pulmonary vein is isolated. The dissection proceeds into the subcarinal space to retrieve station 7 lymph nodes, with the assistant applying pressure on the basilar segments and the surgeon using the fourth arm to push on the superior segments for better exposure. Bleeding occurs during the subcarinal lymph node dissection, managed with a sponge roll to absorb blood.

The surgeon uses fingertips of gloves to retrieve subcarinal lymph node tissue, avoiding excessive dissection to minimize bleeding. Fibrillar is packed into the area for hemostasis. The upper lobe is reflected inferiorly to access paratracheal lymph nodes, with the fourth arm holding the lobe down. The zero-degree camera provides a clear view of the space between the cava, bronchus, and azygos vein, allowing dissection down to the pericardium to remove nodal tissue and fat.

The surgeon reflects the lung posteriorly to expose the medial hilum, isolating the superior pulmonary vein and identifying the middle lobe vein. A vessel loop is placed around the middle lobe vein without dividing it to avoid congestion. The surgeon attempts to expose the pulmonary artery within the fissure, noting the presence of a thick fissure. A station XI lymph node is dissected near the interlobar pulmonary artery, with some bleeding managed using sponge rolls. The fourth arm of the robotic system is used to elevate the middle lobe, while sponge rolls assist in blunt dissection and blood absorption.

The surgeon dissects the middle lobe artery and prepares to divide the medial aspect of the major fissure to expose hilar structures. Bronchiolymph nodes are dissected off the middle lobe bronchus for better exposure. The surgeon uses a Rob Nell catheter to safely guide the stapler across the fissure, ensuring the pulmonary artery is protected during division.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to hold the middle lobe up and away, providing a clear view of the middle lobe vein, bronchus, and artery. The middle lobe vein is divided in two parts to ensure venous drainage to the upper lobe is preserved. The middle lobe bronchus is visible coursing underneath the interlobar pulmonary artery. The surgeon dissects the medial aspect of the middle lobe bronchus, encountering a vascular bronchial lymph node, which is identified as involved with N-one disease.

The surgeon is positioned underneath the middle lobe bronchus, preparing to divide it using a green load stapler, with a Robnell catheter protecting the middle lobe artery. The peribronchial lymph node is visible and appears inflamed and vascular. The surgeon then tackles the middle lobe artery, using a gray load stapler for division. The minor fissure is examined, with the tumor encroaching upon it, prompting the surgeon to adjust the margin towards the upper lobe using a SureForm stapler for a clean staple line.

The surgeon divides the lung parenchyma, aligning it with the vein to ensure hilar structures are outside the staple line. Connective tissue tethering the lobe to the superior pulmonary vein is divided, and a small venous branch is clipped using medium metal clips. The surgeon uses a 60mm stapler to excise the middle lobe, reflecting the lung to find the best angle for stapling. The middle lobe is detached and placed in an anchor bag for removal from the chest.
